task: Explain chunking strategies and which is best for small corpora.

result.query: chunking strategies and best for small corpora in RAG systems

results.title: RAG Overview
results.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines a retriever and a generator. The retriever finds relevant documents from a vector store. The generator uses retrieved context to produce grounded answers. RAG reduces hallucinations because the model references real documents.

results.title: Embedding Models
results.text: Sentence transformers convert text into dense vectors. all-MiniLM-L6-v2 produces 384-dimensional embeddings. Cosine similarity ranks retrieved documents by relevance.

results.title: Evaluation Metrics
results.text: Precision at K measures relevant results in top-K. Recall at K measures relevant documents retrieved. Hallucination rate counts unsupported statements. End-to-end latency equals retrieval plus generation latency.

results.title: Agent Controllers
results.text: An agent controller orchestrates multiple tools to complete multi-step tasks. Tool selection can be rule-based or LLM-driven. Transparency in decisions is critical for debugging.

steps.input: Chunking strategies for RAG systems and their performance in small corpora

results.title: Chunking Strategies
results.text: Fixed-size chunking splits documents into equal-length segments with overlap. Smaller chunks give precise retrieval. Larger chunks keep more context. 512 characters with 64 overlap is a strong default.

results.title: MLOps and RAG in Production
results.text: Production RAG needs monitoring of retrieval quality and latency. Hybrid search combines dense and BM25 sparse retrieval. Logging every step is essential for debugging production systems.

results.title: RAG Overview
results.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines a retriever and a generator. The retriever finds relevant documents from a vector store. The generator uses retrieved context to produce grounded answers. RAG reduces hallucinations because the model references real documents.

steps.input: Retrieved information on chunking strategies in the context of RAG systems and FAISS. Chunking is a technique used to break down the retrieved documents into smaller, more manageable pieces (chunks) before being input into the language model for generation. This improves efficiency by allowing the model to process fewer tokens and focus on specific aspects of the text. There are different chunking strategies like sliding window, sentence-based, and passage-based. For small corpora, sentence-based chunking is often preferred due to its simplicity and efficiency.
steps.reasoning: Summarized the retrieved information about chunking strategies in RAG systems for a clearer explanation.

result.summary: Chunking strategies in RAG systems and FAISS involve breaking down retrieved documents into smaller pieces (chunks) for more efficient processing by language models, allowing the model to focus on specific aspects of the text. Strategies include sliding window, sentence-based, and passage-based, with sentence-based being particularly useful for small corpora.

steps.input: Explain chunking strategies and which is best for small corpora.
steps.result:
In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, chunking strategies are employed to break down retrieved documents into smaller pieces, or chunks, for more efficient processing by language models. This allows the model to focus on specific aspects of the text, improving performance.

For small corpora, sentence-based chunking seems to be particularly useful due to its ability to capture the context of individual sentences effectively. However, it's important to note that different strategies might work best depending on the specific task and data at hand.

In RAG systems using FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search), the commonly used chunking strategy is all-MiniLM-L6-v2 which converts text into 384-dimensional dense vectors, and cosine similarity is used to rank retrieved documents by relevance.

final_answer:
In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, chunking strategies are employed to break down retrieved documents into smaller pieces, or chunks, for more efficient processing by language models. This allows the model to focus on specific aspects of the text, improving performance.

For small corpora, sentence-based chunking seems to be particularly useful due to its ability to capture the context of individual sentences effectively. However, it's important to note that different strategies might work best depending on the specific task and data at hand.

In RAG systems using FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search), the commonly used chunking strategy is all-MiniLM-L6-v2 which converts text into 384-dimensional dense vectors, and cosine similarity is used to rank retrieved documents by relevance.
